summary.brief: Doull v. Foster. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Between 2008 and 2011, Laura Doull was a patient of Anna C. Foster, a nurse practitioner, and her supervisor, Dr. Richard J. Miller
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In August 2008, Doull had an appointment with Foster to seek advice regarding perimenopause-related symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Foster prescribed Doull a topically applied, naturally derived progesterone cream to treat the symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Doull continued to use the progesterone cream through the spring of 2011.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In May 2011, Doull had a 'seizure-like event' and was transported to the hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: At the hospital, she was diagnosed with a pulmonary embolism.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In 2015, Doull died from complications arising from CTEPH.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Paul Genecin, a primary care internal medicine physician and the plaintiffs' expert witness, testified that natural progesterone was not any safer than synthetic derivations of the hormone.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Paul Genecin

evidence_index.text: Dr. Nicholas S. Hill, a pulmonologist and an expert for the defense, testified that there was 'no evidence anywhere that indicates that progesterone cream applied to the skin increases the risk of clotting.'
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Nicholas S. Hill

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a verdict for the defendants and answered various special questions.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury did find that Foster negligently failed to diagnose Doull's pulmonary embolism, they found that this negligence was not the cause of either the harms suffered by Doull after her seizure-like event in 2011 or her death in 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the but-for standard was the appropriate standard for factual causation.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient had a history of asthma and allergies. She visited the medical practice on three occasions in spring 2011 complaining of shortness of breath.

focused_exam.respiratory: Examination findings not specified, but shortness of breath was noted

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Shortness of breath attributed to asthma and allergies

requestables.name: Blood tests
requestables.description: Routine blood tests to check for clotting disorders

reveal.decision_impact: May prompt further investigation for pulmonary embolism

requestables.name: Lung scan
requestables.description: Imaging to check for pulmonary embolism

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with shortness of breath

key_events.event: Patient visits medical practice three times for shortness of breath

timeline_phases.name: Hospitalization and Diagnosis
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences seizure-like event and is hospitalized

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient presenting with shortness of breath
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in managing this patient's shortness of breath?

options.description: Order a lung scan to check for pulmonary embolism
options.clinical_reasoning: Shortness of breath could be due to a pulmonary embolism, especially given the chronic nature of symptoms.

options.description: Continue treating for asthma and allergies
options.clinical_reasoning: Assumed shortness of breath was due to existing asthma and allergies.

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued treating for asthma and allergies

expected_action_court.description: A lung scan should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated that a lung scan was necessary to rule out pulmonary embolism

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to investigate potential pulmonary embolism despite symptoms
explanation.what_should_have_happened: A lung scan should have been performed to diagnose or rule out pulmonary embolism
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed CTEPH and died from complications

key_findings: Jury found negligence in failure to diagnose but no causation

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Not applicable as causation was not established

factual_timeline.event: Patient visits medical practice for shortness of breath
Patient consults with Foster for perimenopause symptoms
Patient experiences seizure-like event and is diagnosed with pulmonary embolism

court_findings.finding: Defendant negligently failed to diagnose pulmonary embolism, but this was not the cause of the harm
No causation established despite finding of negligence
